1: The scene shifts to Belshazzar's feast. Belshazzar was historically the son of Nabonidus (556-539 BCE), the last Neo-Babylonian king, who served as co-regent while his father campaigned in Arabia. The Nabonidus Chronicle and cuneiform administrative texts confirm Belshazzar's role. The feast may coincide with the fall of Babylon to Cyrus; the Cyrus Cylinder records entry into the city on October 12, 539 BCE.
2: "While Belshazzar tasted the wine" — the desecration of the temple vessels is committed in drunken bravado. The vessels had been seized by Nebuchadnezzar (Daniel 1:2; 2 Chronicles 36:10). Their use in a feast praising Babylonian idols represents the maximum possible sacrilege — instruments consecrated for YHWH's worship profaned in service of rival deities.
3: The vessels from "the temple of God in Jerusalem" (min hekla di-beyt-elaha di birushlem) are now serving wine at Belshazzar's feast. The chapter is the structural mirror image of Daniel 1: where Daniel refused to defile himself with the king's food, Belshazzar defiles the holy with his own. The chiasm is theological as well as literary.
4: "They praised the gods of gold and silver, of bronze, iron, wood, and stone" — a list of idolatrous materials that echoes the materials of Nebuchadnezzar's dream-statue (Daniel 2:31-33: gold, silver, bronze, iron). The empire worships its own power, projected onto material representations of divinity.
5: "The fingers of a human hand appeared and wrote on the plaster of the wall, opposite the lampstand" — the lampstand illuminates the writing, making it visible to the entire court. The hand comes "from before him" (Daniel 5:24 — min yadeh, divine passive). This is an act of pure divine inscription without human intermediary.
6: "The king's face changed color" (ziv anpohui eshtenoh, "the light of his face was altered") — the same idiom as Daniel 3:19 (Nebuchadnezzar) and Daniel 5:10 (the queen). "The joints of his hips gave way and his knees knocked together" — ANE idiom for terror in the face of divine power (cf. Isaiah 21:3; Jeremiah 30:6; Nahum 2:10).
7: The reward offered — purple robe, gold chain, third rank (talti b'malkuta) — is historically precise. Nabonidus was king, Belshazzar co-regent, so the highest available appointment was third rank. This detail would only be accurate under the Nabonidus co-regency arrangement, which was unknown to scholars until the discovery of the Nabonidus Chronicle in the 19th century.
8: The failure of the wise men follows the established Daniel pattern (Daniel 2, 4), but here the crisis is more acute: the king needs the writing both read and interpreted. The writing was apparently in an unusual form — not simply illegible but requiring inspired interpretation to decode its meaning from its visible letters.
9: "King Belshazzar was greatly alarmed, his color changed, and his lords were bewildered" (mitbahlin, "terrified, confounded"). The court's collective terror mirrors the king's individual response. The moment of imperial confidence in Daniel 5:1-4 has collapsed entirely; the power dynamic has been reversed by a hand writing on a wall.
10: The queen — likely the queen mother, not Belshazzar's wife (who is already present at the feast) — enters with knowledge the king lacks. Her awareness of Daniel's abilities from Nebuchadnezzar's era provides the narrative bridge to Daniel's appearance and reminds the reader that Daniel's reputation has persisted across the dynasty even when not immediately useful.
11: "There is a man in your kingdom in whom is the spirit of the holy gods" — the queen uses the same language Nebuchadnezzar used (Daniel 4:8-9). "In the days of your father" (bi-yomei avuk) uses av (father/ancestor) loosely — in Semitic convention, royal predecessors are often called "father." Nebuchadnezzar was not Belshazzar's direct biological father, but he was the dynasty's founding figure.
12: Daniel is described as having "an excellent spirit, knowledge, and the ability to interpret dreams, explain riddles, and solve difficult problems" (mefera' kirin umeshre' qutrin) — three capabilities precisely suited to the present crisis. The queen's knowledge of Daniel's reputation has been maintained across regime changes; his gift was recognized and remembered even when not in current use.
13: "Are you that Daniel, one of the exiles of Judah?" frames Daniel as a foreigner and deportee — min benei galuta di yehud, "from the sons of the exile of Judah." The repeated emphasis on Daniel's outsider status underscores that his power of interpretation comes from the God of his exile, not from training in Babylonian wisdom traditions.
14: "Light, understanding, and excellent wisdom" (nehiru usoklethanu wehokhmah yatirta) — a wisdom triad. The Aramaic nehiru (light, illumination) may carry irony: Daniel was given the Babylonian name Belteshazzar, which Nebuchadnezzar linked to the light of his god (Daniel 4:8). The true light of divine wisdom now belongs to the one bearing that name.
15: The repeated failure of the wise men is again noted. The pattern is established so firmly by this point in the book that the reader anticipates the outcome before the wise men even appear. The structure teaches by repetition: human divinatory systems, however sophisticated, cannot access the meaning of divine action in history.
16: The offer of rewards is again made. Daniel's response in Daniel 5:17 will be to decline while still providing the interpretation — separating divine service from material reward. This is a consistent pattern: Daniel 2:16 (asks for time without seeking payment), Daniel 5:17 (declines the reward), Daniel 6 (continues praying despite the decree). The gifts of God are not for sale.
17: "Let your gifts remain with you and give your rewards to another" — Daniel's refusal of payment mirrors Elisha's refusal of Naaman's gift (2 Kings 5:16). The interpretation belongs to God, not to a transaction. This separates the prophetic role from the court-diviner role: the court diviner is hired for results; the prophet speaks whether paid or not.
18: Daniel begins with a historical lesson — what God did to Nebuchadnezzar (Daniel 5:18-21) — before applying the principle to Belshazzar. The rhetorical strategy establishes the pattern (God gives and removes kingdoms) before pronouncing the verdict. Belshazzar had access to this lesson; his culpability rests on available knowledge willfully ignored (Daniel 5:22).
19: "Whom he would, he killed, and whom he would, he kept alive; whom he would, he raised up, and whom he would, he humbled." The fourfold description of absolute royal power establishes the height from which Nebuchadnezzar fell and from which Belshazzar will now fall. Even maximum human power operates within divine permission.
20: "But when his heart was lifted up and his spirit became arrogant, he was deposed from his royal throne and his glory was taken from him" — the Nebuchadnezzar narrative compressed into one verse, serving as the principle underlying the judgment now about to be applied. The repeated pattern across Daniel 3, 4, and 5 is the book's central theological argument: human pride that displaces divine acknowledgment ends in humiliation.
21: Daniel summarizes Nebuchadnezzar's madness and restoration: "until he knew that the Most High God rules the kingdom of men and sets over it whom he will." The purpose clause is the key. Nebuchadnezzar eventually learned this; the question of Daniel 5 is whether Belshazzar will learn it before or after the decree executes — and the answer, given that the writing is already on the wall, is effectively: after.
22: "And you, Belshazzar his son, have not humbled your heart, even though you knew all this" (la hashpelt lebbabak kol-qobel di kol-denah yedast). The Aramaic is emphatic: "all of this you knew." The indictment turns on knowledge — Belshazzar had access to the lesson of Nebuchadnezzar and chose to disregard it. Greater knowledge creates greater accountability.
23: "But you have lifted yourself up against the Lord of heaven" (hitromamta 'al mare shemayah) — the preposition 'al (against) is used of hostile action. The vessels desecration is the evidence: using what belongs to YHWH to praise "gods of gold and silver, of bronze, iron, wood, and stone, which do not see or hear or know" — a direct allusion to the idol polemic of Isaiah 44:9-20 and Jeremiah 10:1-16.
24: "So the hand was sent from his presence, and this writing was inscribed" — the passive construction conceals the divine agent. The hand comes from the divine presence (min yadeh). God writes his own verdict directly into the physical environment; no prophet intermediary is required.
25: The inscription: MENE MENE TEKEL UPARSIN. These are Aramaic words with a secondary wordplay as monetary denominations: MENE = mina, TEKEL = shekel, PARSIN = two half-shekels. The denominations descend in value, possibly encoding a message about a kingdom of diminishing worth. The Aramaic consonants could be read multiple ways, which may explain why the court wise men could not interpret it even if they could read the letters.
26: MENE: "God has numbered the days of your kingdom and brought it to an end." The divine passive — God is the counter — is the point. The kingdom is not merely ending through historical contingency; it has been consciously counted out, assessed, and found to have reached its divinely appointed limit. The same sovereignty over historical timing that Daniel proclaimed in Daniel 2 is here executed in real time.
27: TEKEL: "you have been weighed in the balance and found wanting" (chaser). The scales (moznayim) as a metaphor for divine moral assessment appear in Job 31:6 and Isaiah 40:12-15. The verdict is a single word: deficient. Measured against the standard of Nebuchadnezzar's eventual acknowledgment of God, Belshazzar comes up short.
28: PERES: "your kingdom is divided and given over to the Medes and Persians." The wordplay is explicit: peres (to divide/split) sounds like Paras (Persia). The message embedded in the inscription announces the succeeding empire — as Daniel's earlier visions had already mapped (Daniel 2 and 7: the silver kingdom follows the gold).
29: Belshazzar honors Daniel despite the verdict — purple robe, gold chain, third rank — which are now meaningless, since that very night the kingdom ends. The purple and gold given to the "third ruler" of a kingdom that no longer exists that evening is the book's darkest irony: worldly reward given and received in the same breath as the verdict that ends the world that produced it.
30: "That very night Belshazzar the Chaldean king was killed" — confirmed historically. The Nabonidus Chronicle records that Gubaru (Gobryas), Cyrus's commander, entered Babylon without battle. The Cyrus Cylinder describes the peaceful surrender of the city. Belshazzar's death is consistent with the historical record of the city's fall. The narrative's one-sentence brevity mirrors the divine economy: what God declares, he executes without elaboration.
31: "Darius the Mede received the kingdom, being about sixty-two years old." This is the most historically contested verse in Daniel. No "Darius the Mede" between Belshazzar and Cyrus appears in extra-biblical sources. Proposed identifications include Gubaru (Gobryas, Cyrus's general who administered Babylon), Cyrus himself under a different title, or a scribal confusion. The book's four-empire scheme (Dan 2 and 7) requires a Median ruler between Babylon and Persia; whether this reflects historical fact or a schematized tradition remains an open question in scholarship.
